cards.title: What a hook actually is
cards.front: If the offer is the bait, what's the hook?
cards.back: A hook is your marketing message — the front-facing message prospects see before becoming customers. It intrigues them and is the primary reason they take your offer. Think: customer = fish, hook = marketing message, offer/bait = the offer. The hook is a 'mental appetizer.' A great Facebook ad always starts with a great hook.
cards.example: "Is breakfast REALLY the most important meal of the day?" pulls you in before any product is mentioned.

cards.title: Hook is NOT the offer
cards.front: How many hooks can one offer have?
cards.back: Hook does not equal Offer. The offer is the action you want (download lead magnet, buy product); the hook is how you sell/market it. ONE offer can have TEN hooks if you're testing ten messages. Creative should convey the hook, not just show the product.
cards.example: One cheat-sheet offer, tested with 10 different curiosity, pain, and desire angles.

cards.title: Lead with value and a quick win
cards.front: What should you give before you ever make the offer?
cards.back: Lead with value and a quick win before selling. Deliver an immediate 'aha moment' and a real quick win first; only THEN make the offer. (James the guitarist proves four chords play almost any song, gives a volunteer a 5-minute win, THEN offers.)
cards.example: Prove four chords play almost any song, give a 5-minute win, then offer the course.

cards.title: The most important line in a Facebook ad
cards.front: Which single line of a Facebook ad matters most?
cards.back: The first line of post copy is the most important line in a Facebook ad — it's the first words seen in the newsfeed. Open it with a question that addresses your hook, then restate that question as a benefit statement in the headline: same theme, two ways, two places. (13 Elements — Element 5: Ask Questions.)
cards.example: First line: "Looking for an easy way to get more superfoods?" Headline: "Get all your healthy superfoods in one drink."

cards.title: UPSYD — Awareness Staircase
cards.front: What 5 steps does every prospect climb before buying?
cards.back:
UPSYD is a five-step awareness continuum to match every hook to where the prospect sits:
- U — Unaware (bottom): unaware of the problem
- P — Problem Aware: knows the problem, not the solution
- S — Solution Aware: knows solutions/companies exist (the 'low-hanging fruit')
- Y — Your-Solution Aware: knows your specific solution/product name
- D — Deal (top): ready to buy on an offer
Moving up one step is easy; skipping a step is harder; jumping 3-4 steps is impossible — it becomes a leap, not a step.
cards.example: An Unaware audience can't jump straight to 'Deal' — they need education first.

cards.title: The UPSYD start-step tradeoff
cards.front: Why start lower on the awareness staircase if it's more work?
cards.back: Key tradeoff: the higher you start, the more directly you can go for the sale — but the audience is smaller. The lower you start, the more value/education you must provide — but it scales far better because you can target large, broad audiences and let Facebook's algorithm work. Use targeting to find the audience's step, then craft messaging to move them up the remaining steps.
cards.example: Solution-aware hooks convert fast but cap out; unaware-friendly hooks scale to huge audiences.

cards.title: Niche the content, target broad
cards.front: Are the riches really in the niches?
cards.back: This contradicts 'the riches are in the niches.' Niche down WITHIN the ad's content, but target big and broad for better long-term results. Copy fit: Unaware/Problem-aware → long-copy ad that makes them aware of the problem, then transitions to your solution. Solution-aware → simpler short-copy ad referencing the known problem.
cards.example: Narrow, specific message inside the ad; broad audience setting so the algorithm can optimize.

cards.title: STEPPS — Six Principles of Sharing
cards.front: What 6 levers make a hook get shared (and the algorithm reward it)?
cards.back:
STEPPS (Jonah Berger, Contagious) — leverage as many as possible to maximize shares:
- S — Social Currency: we share things that make us look good
- T — Triggers: top of mind is tip of tongue
- E — Emotions: when we care, we share
- P — Public Visibility: when something is built to show, it's built to grow
- P — Practical Value: something the audience can actually use
- S — Stories: information shared in an entertaining manner
cards.example: A breakfast hook (Triggers) re-triggers every time someone sits down to eat breakfast.

cards.title: Knowledge Gap hook
cards.front: How do you create an 'itch' a prospect must scratch?
cards.back: The Knowledge Gap hook creates a gap in the prospect's knowledge — an itch they must scratch by reading on. Works best when combined with a counter-intuitive statement.
cards.example: "Is breakfast REALLY the Most Important Meal of the Day?"

cards.title: Curiosity building block (#1)
cards.front: Three quick ways to manufacture curiosity in a hook?
cards.back:
Building Block #1 — Curiosity: curiosity-based hooks grab attention and keep people reading/watching. Techniques:
- Ask a thought-provoking question
- Use a shocking statement
- Contradict conventional wisdom
cards.example: Contradicting conventional wisdom: "Should breakfast actually be skipped?"

cards.title: Give an Aha Moment (#5)
cards.front: Which hook type is HUGE for the algorithm and ROI?
cards.back: Building Block #5 — Give an 'Aha Moment': reveal an insight or expose a common belief as a myth/inaccurate/recently-changed. Builds instant trust, makes the ad far more shareable, and is HUGE for the algorithm — cheaper clicks/impressions/views, more impressions, and higher ROI.
cards.example: Exposing a widely-held belief as a myth to spark shares.

cards.title: Call out their challenge or desire (#2)
cards.front: Where should the opening line's pain or desire come from?
cards.back: Building Block #2 — Challenge/Frustration/Pain/Desire: call out the audience's biggest challenge or desire in the opening line, sourced VERBATIM from surveys. Use two deep-dive survey questions. References: Ryan Levesque's Ask and Glen Livingston's survey/quiz material.
cards.example: Pull the opening line straight from a survey response, almost word for word.

cards.title: Speak to the want, not the need
cards.front: What do you hook with vs. what do you deliver?
cards.back: Speak to the want, not the need. Hook with what they emotionally WANT (play their favorite song); deliver what they NEED in the paid product.
cards.example: Hook: "Play your favorite song." Deliver: proper technique and theory in the paid course.

cards.title: Charged desire + measurable outcome
cards.front: Why does 'drive the ball ten yards farther' beat vague benefits?
cards.back: Pair an emotionally-charged desire with a specific, measurable outcome — it taps ego/bragging rights AND promises a defined result. Benchmark: of 100 golfers asked their #1 want, more than 1 in 5 (20%+) say 'drive the ball farther.' Specific outcome examples: 'ten yards farther' / '10 strokes off in <30 days.'
cards.example: "Drive the ball ten yards farther" beats "improve your golf game."

cards.title: Specificity + immediacy
cards.front: Should a hook promise a big long-term result or a tiny immediate one?
cards.back: Specificity + immediacy: deliver a super-specific, immediate result, not a broad/long-term one. Find the one tiny thing that compels the click.
cards.example: "How to sell from the stage" beats "build a six-figure coaching practice."

cards.title: Move away from pain (loss aversion)
cards.front: How much more powerful is avoiding a loss than gaining a win?
cards.back: Move away from pain: people do more to avoid pain than gain pleasure (Robbins). Losses are about 2x as psychologically powerful as equivalent gains (Tversky & Kahneman — it's better not to lose $5 than to find $5). Ask the 3 pain questions, then embed your solution inside the hook and cure it with your offer.
cards.example: Framing the cost of NOT fixing the problem hits ~2x harder than promising the upside.

cards.title: Embed the solution inside the hook
cards.front: What makes a hook specific enough to convert?
cards.back: A great hook is specific to a narrow avatar with the solution embedded in the problem it agitates. Specificity beats generic phrasing.
cards.example: "How to reliably track your Facebook leads, sales, and ROI for your customers" beats generic "how to get results with Facebook."

cards.title: Stop the Scroll — 3 video tactics
cards.front: What 3 tricks stop the thumb in the first 3-10 seconds of a video ad?
cards.back:
Three-Step Video Ad Formula, Step 1 — Stop the Scroll. The first 3-10 seconds are vital (autoplay sound-on is an opportunity, not a guarantee). Three proven tactics:
- Blatant hand motions — wave at the viewer, pull them toward you like a store demonstrator
- Text overlay/captions — catches sound-off viewers and delivers the message faster (we read faster than we speak)
- Pattern interrupt — make the picture look unusual (movement, extreme close-up, selfie-style look)
cards.example: The Squatty Potty pooping-unicorn video is an extreme pattern interrupt — you don't need anything that outrageous.

cards.title: Why the Ketogenic hook works — 4 criteria
cards.front: What 4 boxes does a great contrarian hook check?
cards.back:
Why 'Is breakfast really the most important meal of the day?' works — 4 criteria:
- Curiosity-based and contrarian
- Shatters a conventional myth held by millions (that breakfast is the most important meal)
- Educates/informs on something new and interesting
- Speaks to the avatar's pain point (never has time to eat breakfast, feels guilty about it)
cards.example: All four boxes checked in one short question.

cards.title: What makes a great offer
cards.front: What 5 traits define a great (free) offer?
cards.back:
A great offer is:
- Highly specific
- Easily consumable
- Provides immediate results when implemented
- Speaks to a known desire
- Incomplete relative to the end product (leaves them wanting the paid product)
cards.example: A specific, quick-win cheat sheet that solves one thing but points to the full course.

cards.title: Move Toward Desire — 3 questions
cards.front: What 3 questions surface the 'desire behind the desire'?
cards.back:
Move Toward Desire — 3 desire questions:
- What are our prospect's desires?
- What does our prospect want more than anything else?
- What do we have in our product that moves them toward that one thing they want most?
Then repeatedly ask 'why do they really want this?' to reach the desire behind the desire, then identify the end benefit.
cards.example: Wants to play Van Halen → why? → impress friends → why? → feel admired.

cards.title: Move Away From Pain — 3 questions
cards.front: What 3 questions uncover the pain to cure with your offer?
cards.back:
Move Away From Pain — 3 pain questions:
- What are your target market's greatest problems? (What keeps them up at night?)
- What are they most afraid of?
- Why do they want to change?
Then embed your solution in the hook and cure it with your offer.
cards.example: Up at night about wasted ad spend → embed 'stop wasting money' in the hook.

cards.title: Desire + Benefit Hook Formula
cards.front: What fill-in-the-blank formula links desire to its payoff?
cards.back:
Desire + Benefit Hook Formula:
"They want [specific desire] so that they can [benefit]."
[specific desire] = what they want more than anything else; [benefit] = why they want it / the end benefit. Write out about a dozen of these and keep going (even if bad at first) until you find the perfect match.
cards.example: "They want to track every lead so that they can take on more customers."

cards.title: If/Then Hook Statement
cards.front: What template turns a solution into a benefit-led hook?
cards.back:
If/Then Hook Statement:
"If only [solution]... Then [benefit]."
The 'if' reveals the solution (or desire/pain); the 'then' reveals the benefit. Make sure the solution you agitate is embedded in your own offer/cheat sheet.
cards.example: "If only there was a reliable system to simplify all the setup and analysis. Then that would let me take on more customers." → "how to reliably track your Facebook leads, sales, and ROI for your customers."

cards.title: Question first line → benefit headline
cards.front: How do you pair the post's first line with the headline?
cards.back:
Question-first-line → benefit-headline pattern (Element 5): open the first line with a question addressing the hook, then restate it as a benefit in the headline.
- First line of post copy: "Looking for an easy way to get more superfoods?"
- Headline: "Get all your healthy superfoods in one drink."
Reuse the same ad for cold traffic, retargeting, image ads, and slideshow ads.
cards.example: Same theme, two ways, two places — question then benefit.

cards.title: Two deep-dive survey questions
cards.front: What two survey questions feed verbatim hook copy?
cards.back:
Two deep-dive survey questions for Building Block #2:
- Frustration/Challenge: "What's your number-one single biggest challenge or frustration with [topic] right now? (Be detailed and specific — go beyond 'too confusing' or 'too expensive.')"
- Desire: "Why do you want to [achieve goal]? What is the biggest reason why you want to master this or have someone master it for you?"
Pull opening lines verbatim; responses are often nearly identical with slight variation.
cards.example: Verbatim line pulled: "scaling your business with max ROI and minimum wasted money."

cards.title: Google the hook (research method)
cards.front: How do you find hooks when you don't know the market?
cards.back: Google the hook: search a question keyword (e.g., 'intermittent fasting questions' → ~500,000 results signals interest), read FAQ/expert articles for contrarian angles, search a problem phrase, and use the Google suggestion tool to surface the most popular searches as hook roots. Borrow the hook without plagiarizing (as Ogilvy did from Pierce-Arrow). Essential when you don't know the market/avatar; still uncovers unknown pains/desires for known markets.
cards.example: "Intermittent fasting questions" returning ~500k results signals strong market interest.

cards.title: 7 Power Questions — Q1: Novelty
cards.front: Why are humans biologically wired to notice the unfamiliar?
cards.back: 7 Power Questions, Q1 — Make the offer novel, unique, and distinctive. Humans are biologically wired to give focused attention to the unfamiliar (survival instinct). If the offer isn't actually new, ask: (1) How can it feel new and noteworthy? (2) How can it be made new? (3) What's a different angle? (4) Are there current news stories to attach it to? Proof: 'Major announcement' is one of the best email open-rate subject lines; cable news exploits this with 'Breaking News' banners.
cards.example: "Major announcement" as a subject line signals the new/unfamiliar and lifts open rates.

cards.title: 7 Power Questions — Q3: Commitment & consistency
cards.front: How does asking about identity boost commitment before the ask?
cards.back: 7 Power Questions, Q3 — Open with a question that triggers commitment & consistency (Cialdini, Pre-Suasion, 2016). General questions engage the mind, not identity. Better questions make prospects affirm something about THEMSELVES (Are you brave? helpful?), then point that self-image at the desired action. Caution: choose questions whose honest 'yes' QUALIFIES the prospect; can become manipulative if pushed too far. Benchmark: street-survey completion rose +70% by opening with an identity question vs. a direct ask.
cards.example: "Do you consider yourself brave?" → "Yes" → "Then are you brave enough to end the schism between sales and marketing?"

cards.title: Identity / commitment-consistency opener
cards.front: What opener gets a 'yes' that qualifies the prospect first?
cards.back:
Identity / commitment-consistency opener:
"Pardon me — do you consider yourself to be a helpful person? Great! If so, I can really use your help..."
Or: "Do you consider yourself brave? → Then are you brave enough to end one of the biggest schisms in business between sales and marketing?" This identity opener raised street-survey completion +70% vs. a direct ask.
cards.example: Affirm a self-image first, then point it at the desired action.

cards.title: Four buyer personalities
cards.front: Which 4 buyer types live inside every audience?
cards.back:
Different angles for different buyer personalities — within any audience hit:
- 'I want the best' (competitive/egotistical)
- Impulse buyers
- Process-driven/methodical/logic-based
- Gut-feel/emotion-based people
Each needs different messaging, ad types, angles, and hooks.
cards.example: The logic-driven buyer needs proof and process; the impulse buyer needs a fast, exciting hook.

cards.title: Three Hooks / Headlines / Videos
cards.front: Why bet on three angles instead of one?
cards.back: Three Hooks / Three Headlines / Three Videos: use three of each instead of one to tap multiple specific success drivers rather than betting on a single angle. (Michael Smart's PR training promised a 24-hour boost on reporter pitches.)
cards.example: 3 hooks x 3 headlines x 3 videos surfaces winners faster than a single guess.

cards.title: Creative must convey the hook
cards.front: Should your image just show the product nicely?
cards.back: Creative must convey the hook, not just show the product or 'something that looks good.' Also play off emotions/associations already in the culture — borrow pre-loaded emotion from icons people already relate to (low iPhone battery or low bank account → portrays scarcity).
cards.example: A report-card 'F' image beat the lead-magnet mockup because it carried the hook.

cards.title: Desire-behind-the-desire ad copy
cards.front: How do you layer surface want, pain, and deeper payoff in copy?
cards.back:
Desire-behind-the-desire ad copy (Van Halen avatar): stack the surface desire, the named outcome, the specific pain, and the deeper end benefit:
- "Want to play those Van Halen songs you've always wanted to play ever since you were in high school?"
- "Learn how to play like Eddie Van Halen!"
- "Struggling to play Eruption and can't get the fingering down?"
- "...so you can impress your friends / steal the show at the next family reunion."
cards.example: Surface want → named outcome → specific pain → deeper payoff (admiration).

cards.title: Pitfall: framing skill as a mountain
cards.front: Why does citing the 10,000-hour rule kill conversions?
cards.back: Don't frame skill as an intimidating mountain. Emphasizing massive effort (thousands of hours, Gladwell's 10,000-hour mark, rare natural talent) makes prospects feel success is untouchable, so they quit before starting. Instead lower the perceived barrier — 'you just had a little help learning some shortcuts' — and make them believe they can do it too.
cards.example: Avoid the 10,000-hour framing; promise shortcuts instead.

cards.title: Pitfall: leading with the solution name
cards.front: Why can a 'Download your FREE cheat sheet' hook actively repel people?
cards.back: Don't lead with the solution name to broad audiences. A search-style hook like 'Download your FREE Intermittent Fasting cheat sheet now!' only lands on Your-Solution-Aware audiences. On broad/less-aware audiences the term may even repel (e.g., 'fasting' read as a religious ritual) and the message goes over their heads — they need education first.
cards.example: "Fasting" misread as a religious ritual by an unaware audience.

cards.title: Pitfall: generic solution-name hook at scale
cards.front: Why does 'how to flip houses' have a scalability problem?
cards.back: Don't use a generic solution-name hook when you need scale. 'How to flip houses' resonates only with the small solution-aware segment → scalability problem. Use a more specific/curiosity-driven hook to reach the larger, less-aware market.
cards.example: Swap "how to flip houses" for a specific, curiosity-driven angle to scale.

cards.title: Pitfall: leading with credentials or a discount
cards.front: What should never open your ad before you've built relationship?
cards.back: Don't lead with credentials or a discount. Give aha moments, build relationship, and motivate consumption first — rather than opening with your credentials or a hard sell/discount.
cards.example: Open with an insight, not "As a 20-year expert, here's 50% off."

cards.title: Pitfall: creative is just a pretty product shot
cards.front: What happens when the creative looks good but ignores the hook?
cards.back: Don't make the creative just a pretty product shot. If the image/video doesn't convey the hook, it won't carry a high-converting campaign.
cards.example: The report-card 'F' image beat the polished lead-magnet mockup because it conveyed the hook.
